_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology shift since Hib vaccine per Shah et al. JAMA 2010 (PMID 20068221) and Guldfred et al. J Laryngol Otol 2008. Adult predominance and management per Suzuki et al. 2015 and ENT UK guidance. Airway management per Difficult Airway Society and AAOHNS. WHO Hib data sourced from 2024 vaccination coverage report.

h1Title: Epiglottitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Epiglottitis is acute inflammation and rapid swelling of the epiglottis and adjacent supraglottic structures that can occlude the airway within hours. Before routine Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) vaccination in 1985, the disease struck roughly 5 per 100,000 children aged 2-7 each year in the United States; pediatric incidence has since fallen by more than 95% and adults now account for over 70% of cases, with an incidence of 1-4 per 100,000 adults per year. Patients present with a brief 6-24 hour history of severe sore throat, painful swallowing, drooling, muffled (hot-potato) voice, and a preference to sit forward with the neck extended. Untreated, complete airway obstruction develops in 6-10% of cases and mortality reaches 7-20% without intervention. Modern management — intravenous third-generation cephalosporin, controlled airway protection in an operating room, and dexamethasone — reduces mortality below 1%.

keyStats.prevalence: Adults 1-4 per 100,000 per year (US, Europe); pediatric incidence under 0.5 per 100,000 since Hib vaccination
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 2.5:1 in adults; pediatric peak historically 2-7 years, now spread across all ages
keyStats.avgAge: Adults: peak age 40-50 years (Shah et al. 2010); children: rare, typically 6-12 years when it occurs in vaccinated populations
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 15,000-25,000 hospitalizations per year in high-income countries; substantially higher in regions with low Hib vaccine coverage

definition: Epiglottitis (ICD-10: J05.10 without obstruction; J05.11 with obstruction), also called acute supraglottitis, is rapidly progressive inflammation of the epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids, and false vocal cords. The condition typically begins with bacterial seeding of the rich loose connective tissue of the supraglottis, producing edema that can narrow the airway lumen from a normal 6-8 mm to under 2 mm within hours. Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) was the dominant causative organism in the pre-vaccine era, accounting for over 90% of pediatric cases. After universal Hib immunization, the microbiology has shifted: Streptococcus pneumoniae, group A and group B streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus (including methicillin-resistant strains), and non-typeable H. influenzae now predominate, particularly in adults. Non-infectious supraglottitis from thermal injury (steam, smoke, drinking very hot liquids), caustic ingestion, foreign body, post-radiation injury, and allergic angioedema can mimic the picture. The disease is a true otolaryngology and emergency medicine emergency: airway loss is the dominant threat and definitive management is performed jointly by ENT surgery, anesthesia, intensive care, and infectious disease teams. Without airway protection, sudden complete obstruction can occur during examination, intubation attempts, or even patient transport.

typesClassification.description: Now rare in countries with routine Hib vaccination but more abrupt and severe when it occurs. Classic presentation: a previously well 2-7 year-old with 4-12 hours of high fever, drooling, refusal to swallow, and tripod posture. Airway loss can occur within 1-2 hours of presentation.

Now the dominant form. More indolent course (24-72 hours), broader microbiology, and lower rates of complete airway loss (10-15% require intubation versus historically 70-100% in children). Higher relative mortality in those who do obstruct because of delayed recognition.

Caused by thermal injury (inhaled steam, hot foods, crack cocaine), caustic ingestion, foreign body, radiation, or angioedema (hereditary, ACE-inhibitor-induced, anaphylactic). Identical airway risk; antibiotic management is replaced by allergen avoidance, C1-inhibitor concentrate, or bradykinin-receptor antagonists where appropriate.

Repeated episodes in the same patient, usually adult. Associated with immunosuppression, diabetes, structural laryngeal abnormalities, and previous radiotherapy. Investigation includes immune workup and laryngoscopy between episodes.

Localized collection within the epiglottis (intra-epiglottic) or adjacent aryepiglottic fold. Develops in 5-15% of adult cases. Requires drainage in addition to systemic antibiotics; visible on lateral neck imaging and contrast-enhanced CT.

primarySymptoms: Rapidly progressive severe sore throat that is disproportionate to oropharyngeal examination findings, developing over 6-24 hours.
Odynophagia (painful swallowing) and dysphagia severe enough that patients refuse oral intake including their own saliva.
Drooling because swallowing saliva is too painful and the inflamed epiglottis obstructs the entrance to the esophagus.
Muffled, thick, hot-potato voice (dysphonia without true hoarseness), reflecting supraglottic edema rather than vocal-fold pathology.
Inspiratory stridor, neck retraction, and accessory-muscle use signaling impending airway obstruction; absence of stridor does not exclude critical narrowing.
Fever (often 38.5-40 °C in pediatric cases; more variable in adults), tachycardia, and prostration.
Tripod posture — sitting upright, leaning forward, neck extended, jaw thrust, mouth open — adopted instinctively to maximize airway caliber.
Anterior neck tenderness over the hyoid bone and laryngeal cartilages on gentle palpation.
Cervical lymphadenopathy in 30-50% of adult cases.
Anxiety, restlessness, and a sense of impending doom in critically ill patients — late signs of hypoxia.
earlyWarningSigns: Severe sore throat without significant tonsillar or pharyngeal erythema on examination
New muffled voice changing the quality of speech without true hoarseness
Difficulty handling oral secretions or drooling in an awake adult
Preference to sit upright and lean forward rather than lie down
Pain reported as much worse than appearance of the throat suggests
emergencySigns: Inspiratory stridor at rest, suprasternal or intercostal retractions, or visible accessory-muscle use
Drooling with tripod posture in any patient (especially a child)
Cyanosis, falling oxygen saturation, or altered mental status suggesting hypoxia
Sudden silence or quiet stridor in a previously distressed patient (impending arrest)
Inability to lie supine because of airway compromise — do not force a supine position for imaging or intubation outside the operating room

causes.description: Historically the dominant cause, accounting for over 90% of pediatric cases before 1990. Bacteria seed the loose submucosal tissue of the epiglottis, producing rapid edema. Universal infant Hib immunization (introduced 1985-1992 across high-income countries) has reduced this etiology by more than 95%.

Now the most common bacterial cause in adults and partially-vaccinated children. Group A streptococcus is associated with the most fulminant adult cases and the highest rate of epiglottic abscess formation. Group B streptococcus accounts for occasional cases in pregnancy and the elderly.

Increasingly recognized cause in adults, particularly people who inject drugs and those with diabetes. Methicillin-resistant strains drive empiric coverage with vancomycin or linezolid in regions with high MRSA prevalence.

causes.cause: Non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae and other Gram-negative organisms
causes.description: Non-vaccine Haemophilus strains, Moraxella catarrhalis, Klebsiella, and Pseudomonas account for the remainder of adult bacterial epiglottitis, with Klebsiella particularly described in East Asian diabetic populations.

Herpes simplex, Epstein-Barr, and parainfluenza viruses cause occasional cases. Candida albicans supraglottitis occurs in profoundly immunosuppressed patients (HIV, post-transplant, prolonged steroids) and presents with white plaques on laryngoscopy.

Inhalation of steam, very hot liquids, crack cocaine, or caustic substances can produce mechanically identical supraglottic edema. Foreign body impaction, instrumentation, or radiation to the head and neck also cause non-infectious supraglottitis.

ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema, hereditary or acquired C1-inhibitor deficiency, and IgE-mediated anaphylaxis can produce isolated supraglottic swelling indistinguishable from bacterial epiglottitis until laryngoscopy. Antibiotics are ineffective; treatment requires icatibant, C1-inhibitor concentrate, fresh frozen plasma, or epinephrine and steroids depending on mechanism.

riskFactors.description: Children without a complete primary Hib series have an estimated 20-50 fold higher risk of Hib epiglottitis. Adults born before 1985 (and migrants from low-coverage regions) carry residual susceptibility because Hib vaccination has historically targeted infants.

Modern adult epiglottitis peaks in the fifth decade with a 2.5:1 male predominance. Mechanism is unclear but likely combines higher exposure to oropharyngeal pathogens, more comorbidity, and structural laryngeal changes.

Diabetes is present in 15-30% of adult epiglottitis cases versus 8-10% of age-matched controls. Hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil function and ciliary clearance and promotes more severe disease and higher rates of epiglottic abscess.

Increases susceptibility to unusual pathogens (Candida, Pseudomonas, Klebsiella), prolongs course, and raises mortality. Immunosuppressed patients account for a disproportionate share of MRSA and fungal supraglottitis.

Both impair mucosal defense and have been associated with increased adult incidence and severity in Scandinavian and US case series. Heavy alcohol use also predisposes to thermal injury and Klebsiella infection.
Often precedes epiglottitis by 24-48 hours and may damage mucosal barriers, allowing bacterial seeding of the supraglottic tissues.
Damages local lymphatics and microvasculature, predisposing to recurrent supraglottic edema and slower resolution. Patients with prior laryngeal radiation have a 3-5 fold higher recurrence rate.

affectedDemographics: Adults now account for over 70% of cases in high-income countries (Shah et al. JAMA 2010); incidence in US adults 1.0-3.1 per 100,000 per year
Pediatric incidence in countries with universal Hib vaccination has fallen from 3-5 per 100,000 (1980s) to under 0.5 per 100,000 (2020s)
Male predominance roughly 2.5:1 in adults persists across multiple national cohorts
Higher incidence in regions with low Hib vaccine coverage; sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia continue to see classic pediatric Hib epiglottitis
Winter and early spring peaks reported in temperate climates, mirroring upper-respiratory virus seasons
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical and the patient's airway is protected before any investigation that requires lying flat. The classic triad — severe sore throat, drooling, and muffled voice in a patient who looks toxic — should prompt immediate transfer to a setting with airway expertise. Direct fibreoptic laryngoscopy by an experienced ENT surgeon or anesthesiologist is the reference standard and visualizes a swollen, cherry-red, or pale-edematous epiglottis and aryegligottic folds. Lateral soft-tissue neck radiograph (the historical thumb sign) is still useful in stable patients and shows an enlarged epiglottic shadow, but should be deferred in any child or unstable adult because positioning for the film can precipitate obstruction. Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the neck is reserved for suspected abscess or non-infectious supraglottitis once the airway is secured. Blood cultures are drawn before antibiotics; epiglottic swabs are usually deferred because of airway risk. White blood count typically shows neutrophilic leukocytosis with left shift. In children, the differential includes croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis), retropharyngeal abscess, foreign body aspiration, and bacterial tracheitis; in adults, peritonsillar abscess, angioedema, anaphylaxis, and lingual tonsillitis must be excluded. Point-of-care ultrasound of the anterior neck is an emerging adjunct that demonstrates a thickened epiglottis (≥6 mm in adults).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard direct visualization of the epiglottis and supraglottic structures
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by ENT or anesthesia, usually in a controlled setting (resuscitation bay, operating theater, or ICU) with airway equipment ready. The scope is passed through a topically anesthetized nasal passage; the patient remains seated upright. Findings recorded within seconds.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the thumb sign (swollen epiglottis) and vallecular obliteration in stable patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard radiograph taken upright with the neck extended. Avoided in unstable patients and most children because positioning can worsen obstruction. Reported within minutes by a radiologist.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood cultures (two sets) and complete blood count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify causative organism in 10-25% of cases and confirm bacterial infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venipuncture, ideally before the first antibiotic dose. CBC typically returns within an hour; cultures take 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects epiglottic abscess, deep neck space infection, and complications such as mediastinitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed after airway protection in supine position. Takes 10-15 minutes. Intravenous iodinated contrast is given unless contraindicated.

diagnosticTests.test: Point-of-care ultrasound of the anterior neck
diagnosticTests.purpose: Emerging bedside tool to measure epiglottic thickness (over 6 mm supports diagnosis) without moving the patient
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Linear probe placed on the thyroid cartilage with the patient upright; takes under 2 minutes and avoids transport risks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects group A streptococcus, Haemophilus, MRSA when airway is secure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard swab from the posterior pharynx (not the epiglottis itself); molecular results within hours, culture in 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment runs on two parallel tracks: airway protection and antimicrobial therapy, ideally initiated within the first 60 minutes of presentation. Any patient with stridor, drooling, tripod posture, or rapidly progressing symptoms is transferred to an environment with simultaneous ENT, anesthesia, and intensive-care capability; the operating room is preferred. Children and unstable adults undergo controlled inhalational induction with sevoflurane while spontaneous breathing is preserved; muscle relaxants are avoided until a definitive airway is established. Awake fibreoptic intubation is the preferred technique for cooperative adults. A surgeon stands ready to perform emergency cricothyroidotomy or tracheostomy if intubation fails. Empiric antibiotics begin immediately after airway management: intravenous third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone 2 g for adults; 50 mg/kg for children) covers H. influenzae and S. pneumoniae; vancomycin (15-20 mg/kg) or linezolid is added in regions with MRSA prevalence above 10%. Treatment continues intravenously until afebrile and stable, then orally for a total course of 7-10 days. Dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg in children; 10 mg in adults) is widely used to accelerate edema resolution although randomized data remain limited. Epiglottic or supraglottic abscesses larger than 5 mm are drained endoscopically. Extubation is performed in the operating room once the air-leak test is positive and the supraglottis appears decongested on repeat laryngoscopy, typically 24-72 hours after admission. Non-infectious causes (anaphylaxis, angioedema) demand etiology-specific therapy — intramuscular epinephrine, icatibant, or C1-inhibitor concentrate — alongside the same airway-protection principles.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (adults); 50-75 mg/kg IV once daily (children)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line empirical therapy covers H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, and most streptococci. Cefotaxime is an equivalent alternative. Switch to oral co-amoxiclav or cefuroxime once afebrile for 48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution rates of 90-95% in bacterial epiglottitis treated within 24 hours of presentation

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours, or linezolid 600 mg every 12 hours
medicalTreatments.description: Added when MRSA prevalence is above 10%, in injection drug users, and in immunocompromised patients. Trough vancomycin levels of 15-20 mg/L are targeted.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective coverage of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and most resistant Gram-positive supraglottic pathogens

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (max 10 mg) IV, repeated every 6 hours for 24 hours
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces supraglottic edema and may shorten time to extubation. Stop after 24-48 hours to limit immunosuppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data suggest 12-24 hour reduction in intubation duration; no robust randomized evidence in adults

medicalTreatments.name: Nebulized epinephrine (5 mL of 1:1000 solution) and humidified oxygen
medicalTreatments.description: Temporizes airway edema while awaiting definitive intervention. Effect is short-lived (30-90 minutes) and rebound swelling can occur.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful bridge in mild to moderate adult cases; rarely sufficient as sole therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Intramuscular epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg (adult) or 0.01 mg/kg (child)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy when angioedema or anaphylaxis cannot be excluded. Combined with antihistamines and corticosteroids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of anaphylactic airway edema in 70-90% of patients within 15-30 minutes

medicalTreatments.name: Icatibant 30 mg subcutaneous or C1-inhibitor concentrate 20 IU/kg IV
medicalTreatments.description: Specific therapy for hereditary or ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema confirmed by history or laboratory testing. Antibiotics are not effective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief within 30-60 minutes in hereditary angioedema attacks (icatibant); response variable in ACE-inhibitor cases

surgicalOptions.name: Endotracheal intubation under controlled inhalational anesthesia
surgicalOptions.description: Performed in the operating room with ENT surgeon scrubbed for emergency surgical airway. Smaller-than-predicted tube is used because of supraglottic narrowing. Awake fibreoptic intubation preferred in cooperative adults.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful first-pass intubation in 85-95% of cases when performed by an experienced anesthesiologist in a controlled setting

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency cricothyroidotomy or surgical tracheostomy
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for failed intubation or cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate scenarios. Cricothyroidotomy is the fastest rescue technique; conversion to tracheostomy within 24-48 hours is standard.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Airway secured in over 95% of cases performed by trained operators; complication rates 10-20% (bleeding, false passage, late stenosis)

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic drainage of epiglottic or supraglottic abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Performed in the same anesthetic as intubation when abscess is identified on CT. Direct laryngoscopy with needle aspiration or incision and drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution in 80-90% of drained abscesses without recurrence at 6 months

alternativeTreatments.name: Heliox (helium-oxygen mixture)
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduces airway resistance in turbulent flow through narrowed segments and can temporize moderate stridor while definitive management is arranged. Not a substitute for airway protection in critical cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hib booster immunization
alternativeTreatments.description: Indicated in incompletely vaccinated adults who develop Hib epiglottitis and in close contacts. Single dose of conjugate Hib vaccine produces protective antibody in over 95% of recipients.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with suspected epiglottitis requires immediate evaluation in an emergency department capable of summoning ENT and anesthesia together. Outpatient evaluation by a primary care clinician is inappropriate once severe sore throat with drooling, voice change, or stridor is reported; the patient should be transported directly to a hospital with operating-room airway capability.
doctorSelectionGuide: After acute admission, choose an ENT surgeon affiliated with a center that performs at least 50 awake fibreoptic intubations per year and has 24-hour anesthesia and intensive-care backup. Adult patients with recurrent episodes should also be seen by infectious disease and, in selected cases, immunology services.
hospitalCriteria: Onsite ENT surgical service available within 30 minutes
Anesthesia team experienced in awake fibreoptic intubation and pediatric airway
Operating room available for emergency airway
Adult and pediatric intensive-care beds for post-intubation monitoring
Microbiology service with rapid bacterial identification and resistance testing
keyFacilities: Tertiary emergency departments with airway code teams
Adult intensive-care units with bronchoscopy capability
preventionStrategies: Complete the routine Hib infant immunization series (typically 2, 4, 6, and 12-15 months of age) and any catch-up doses recommended by national schedules.
Offer Hib vaccination to unvaccinated adults with asplenia, complement deficiencies, or HIV.
Treat early bacterial pharyngitis or epiglottic abscess promptly to reduce recurrence.
Avoid known triggers in patients with previous thermal supraglottitis (very hot liquids, crack-cocaine inhalation, steam exposure).
Switch from ACE inhibitors to angiotensin-receptor blockers in patients with prior ACE-inhibitor angioedema affecting the airway.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full course of prescribed antibiotics even after symptoms resolve.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol intake to reduce recurrence and accelerate mucosal healing.
Maintain hydration with sips of cool fluids once oral intake is tolerated again.
Avoid very hot liquids and acidic foods for the first 7-10 days after extubation.
Optimize glycemic control if diabetic; aim for HbA1c under 7.5% to reduce recurrence risk.
Carry an emergency medical alert card noting prior epiglottitis and any drug allergies.

recommended: Cool or room-temperature fluids during recovery to soothe the inflamed mucosa
Soft, low-acid foods (yogurt, oatmeal, mashed potato) for the first week after extubation
High-protein foods to support tissue healing
Adequate fluid intake (at least 2 L per day) once swallowing is safe
avoid: Very hot drinks and food during recovery
Acidic foods and beverages (citrus, tomato, vinegar) that aggravate the mucosa
Alcohol while on antibiotics and during recovery
Crusty or sharp foods that mechanically irritate the supraglottis

exerciseGuidelines: Light activity (walking) can resume 48 hours after discharge in uncomplicated cases. Strenuous exercise, swimming, and contact sports should wait until the supraglottis has fully decongested on outpatient laryngoscopy at 2-4 weeks. Patients with tracheostomy follow specific airway-care protocols.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antibiotics at the same times each day to maintain steady drug levels.
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated 30-45 degrees during the first week after discharge.
Monitor temperature twice daily; return immediately if fever above 38.5 °C recurs.
Avoid talking loudly or whispering for the first 7-10 days; both stress the supraglottis.
Use a humidifier in the bedroom to reduce mucosal drying.
Attend the scheduled ENT follow-up at 2-4 weeks for repeat laryngoscopy.
prognosis: Outcome depends almost entirely on the speed of airway protection and antibiotic delivery. Adults reaching a hospital with full airway capability before complete obstruction have mortality rates below 1% and full recovery in over 90% of cases. Pediatric mortality has fallen from approximately 7% in the pre-vaccine era to under 1% with prompt management. Failure to recognize the diagnosis is the strongest predictor of death: case-fatality rises to 7-20% when initial evaluation does not include direct visualization or when intubation is delayed beyond 6 hours of stridor onset. Most survivors require 24-72 hours of intubation; tracheostomy is necessary in 5-15% of adults and under 5% of children. Recurrence occurs in 4-8% of adults, particularly those with diabetes, immunosuppression, or prior radiotherapy. Long-term laryngeal dysfunction, including chronic dysphonia or aspiration, develops in fewer than 5% of survivors.
recoveryTimeline: Intubation is required for a median of 36-48 hours. Most patients are extubated in the operating room once cuff-leak testing is positive and re-laryngoscopy shows decongestion. Hospital stay averages 4-7 days. Symptomatic recovery (normal voice, comfortable swallowing) is complete within 2-3 weeks in over 90% of cases. Outpatient laryngoscopy at 2-4 weeks confirms anatomical resolution.
complications: Complete airway obstruction with cardiorespiratory arrest — the dominant cause of death; risk highest in the first 12 hours and during manipulation
Epiglottic or supraglottic abscess developing in 5-15% of adult cases; suspected when fever persists beyond 48 hours of antibiotics
Aspiration pneumonia from impaired swallowing during the acute phase and immediately post-extubation
Pulmonary edema (post-obstructive negative-pressure pulmonary edema) after relief of severe upper-airway obstruction
Sepsis and bacteremia, particularly in immunosuppressed adults and those with delayed antibiotic delivery
Subglottic stenosis from prolonged intubation, occurring in 2-5% of patients ventilated more than 7 days

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery
supportResources.description: Patient education on laryngeal and supraglottic infections and finding a board-certified otolaryngologist.

supportResources.name: CDC Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) information
supportResources.description: Vaccine schedules, surveillance data, and post-exposure prophylaxis guidance.

supportResources.name: Difficult Airway Society (DAS) guidelines
supportResources.description: Clinician-focused but useful patient-facing resource on awake fibreoptic intubation and emergency airway algorithms.

supportResources.name: WHO Immunization Coverage data — Hib
supportResources.description: Global Hib vaccination coverage statistics and country-level reports.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause acute upper-airway swelling, but anaphylaxis develops within minutes of an allergen exposure (food, drug, insect sting), often with urticaria, wheeze, and hypotension, and responds rapidly to intramuscular epinephrine. Epiglottitis evolves over 6-24 hours with fever and absence of skin or systemic anaphylactic features and requires antibiotics and airway protection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes expiratory wheeze and bronchospasm responsive to inhaled bronchodilators, not the inspiratory stridor and drooling of supraglottic obstruction. Asthma severity scales (peak flow, oxygen saturation, lung auscultation) and the absence of fever, dysphagia, and tripod posture distinguish it clearly. Epiglottitis worsens with bronchodilators if airway already obstructed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sinusitis causes facial pain, post-nasal drip, and nasal obstruction over weeks rather than the acute hours-long course, drooling, and airway compromise of epiglottitis. Imaging shows opacified sinuses without supraglottic edema.

relatedConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
relatedConditions.relevance: Important alternative cause of acute upper-airway edema; epinephrine-responsive rather than antibiotic-responsive.

relatedConditions.name: Laryngeal Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Another laryngeal disease managed by ENT; differential diagnosis in adults with persistent or recurrent voice change and dysphagia.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Common downstream complication after aspiration during the acute phase or post-extubation.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Causes acute breathing difficulty; differs in mechanism (bronchospasm versus supraglottic edema) and treatment.

faqs.question: What is epiglottitis in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Epiglottitis is rapid swelling of the small flap of tissue (the epiglottis) that sits above the voice box. The swelling can close the airway within hours. It is a medical emergency that requires hospital airway protection and intravenous antibiotics.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms include severe sore throat that worsens quickly over 6-24 hours, painful swallowing, drooling, a muffled hot-potato voice, and a preference to sit up and lean forward. Stridor and difficulty breathing develop later. Any of these features needs urgent hospital review.

faqs.question: How do you catch epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Most adult cases are caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, group A streptococci, or Staphylococcus aureus carried in the upper airway. Children in regions without Hib vaccination remain at risk from Haemophilus influenzae type b. Non-infectious cases follow steam burns, chemical inhalation, foreign bodies, or angioedema.

faqs.question: Is epiglottitis contagious?
faqs.answer: The bacteria that cause epiglottitis can spread by respiratory droplets, but the disease itself is rarely transmitted directly. Close household contacts of confirmed Hib cases may be offered rifampin prophylaxis. Casual contact with an infected adult does not require treatment.

faqs.question: How is epiglottitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by direct fibreoptic laryngoscopy performed by an ENT surgeon or anesthesiologist, which shows a swollen red or pale epiglottis. Lateral neck X-ray can show the thumb sign in stable patients. Blood cultures and CT are taken once the airway is secured.

faqs.question: What is the thumb sign in epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: The thumb sign is a swollen, rounded epiglottic shadow on a lateral soft-tissue neck X-ray, resembling an adult thumb. It supports the diagnosis in stable adults but is avoided in unstable patients and small children because positioning for the film can worsen airway obstruction.

faqs.question: How is epiglottitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines airway protection (usually intubation in the operating room) with intravenous antibiotics such as ceftriaxone 2 g daily, and dexamethasone to reduce swelling. Vancomycin is added in regions with high MRSA prevalence. Most adults are extubated in 36-48 hours.

faqs.question: Is epiglottitis fatal?
faqs.answer: Untreated epiglottitis has a mortality of 7-20% because of airway obstruction. With prompt hospital airway management and intravenous antibiotics, mortality falls below 1% in adults and children. Early recognition is the strongest predictor of survival.

faqs.question: Can adults get epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults now account for more than 70% of cases in countries with universal Hib vaccination. Adult epiglottitis develops more slowly than the pediatric form (over 24-72 hours) but carries higher relative mortality if recognition is delayed. Peak age is 40-50 years.

faqs.question: How is pediatric epiglottitis managed differently?
faqs.answer: Children are kept calm in the parent's lap, given supplemental oxygen by face mask, and transferred immediately to the operating room. Examination of the throat with a tongue depressor is avoided because it can precipitate obstruction. Inhalational induction with sevoflurane preserves spontaneous breathing.

faqs.question: Does the Hib vaccine prevent epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: The Hib conjugate vaccine has reduced Haemophilus influenzae type b epiglottitis by more than 95% in countries with universal infant immunization. It does not prevent epiglottitis caused by streptococci, staphylococci, or non-typeable Haemophilus, which now dominate in vaccinated populations.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take?
faqs.answer: Most adults are extubated within 36-48 hours and discharged in 4-7 days. Symptomatic recovery (normal voice and comfortable swallowing) is complete in 2-3 weeks. Outpatient ENT laryngoscopy at 2-4 weeks confirms anatomical healing in over 90% of survivors.

faqs.question: What is the difference between epiglottitis and croup?
faqs.answer: Croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis) typically affects children 6 months to 3 years with a barking cough, hoarseness, and mild stridor lasting days. Epiglottitis affects older children and adults with high fever, drooling, muffled voice, and rapid progression to airway obstruction over hours. Croup responds to steroids and nebulized epinephrine.

faqs.question: Can epiglottitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence occurs in 4-8% of adults, particularly those with diabetes, immunosuppression, alcohol use, or prior head and neck radiotherapy. Investigations after a second episode include immune workup, dental review, and outpatient laryngoscopy to rule out underlying structural causes.

faqs.question: What causes the muffled voice in epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Supraglottic swelling damps the resonance of the larynx without affecting the vocal cords themselves, producing the so-called hot-potato voice. True hoarseness is uncommon. The change in voice quality is one of the earliest and most reliable bedside signs.

faqs.question: Should I try to look at the throat at home?
faqs.answer: No. Pressing a tongue depressor or spoon onto the tongue can precipitate complete airway obstruction in epiglottitis. Keep the patient sitting upright, do not force them to lie down, and call emergency services immediately.

faqs.question: Can angioedema mimic epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema and hereditary C1-inhibitor deficiency can cause isolated supraglottic swelling indistinguishable from bacterial epiglottitis until laryngoscopy. Angioedema usually progresses faster (minutes to hours), without fever, and responds to icatibant, C1-inhibitor concentrate, or epinephrine rather than antibiotics.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: First-line therapy is intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g once daily for adults or 50-75 mg/kg for children. Vancomycin or linezolid is added in regions with high MRSA prevalence. Treatment continues intravenously until afebrile, then orally for a total course of 7-10 days.

faqs.question: Is epiglottitis the same as supraglottitis?
faqs.answer: Many specialists prefer the term supraglottitis because the inflammation typically involves the aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids, and false vocal cords in addition to the epiglottis itself. The two terms are used interchangeably in clinical practice; management is identical.

faqs.question: Do steroids help in epiglottitis?
faqs.answer: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg in children and 10 mg in adults is widely used to reduce supraglottic edema, although randomized evidence is limited. Observational data suggest a 12-24 hour reduction in intubation duration. Steroids are stopped after 24-48 hours to limit immunosuppression.

faqs.question: Will I need a tracheostomy?
faqs.answer: Most patients are managed with short-term endotracheal intubation rather than tracheostomy. Tracheostomy is required in 5-15% of adults and under 5% of children, usually after failed intubation, prolonged ventilation beyond 7-10 days, or anatomical difficulty. Most tracheostomies are reversed within weeks.

metaDescription: Epiglottitis is rapid airway-threatening swelling above the voice box. Symptoms, emergency treatment, vaccination and recovery, medically reviewed.
